serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Japanese curry rice with chicken katsu weighs approximately 500g, comprising roughly 200g steamed white rice, 150g panko-crumbed fried chicken cutlet, and 150g Japanese curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken breast (fried, breaded), cooked; White rice (short-grain, cooked); Curry sauce (commercial, prepared) — composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Japanese curry rice with chicken katsu as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries: (1) fried breaded chicken breast (FDC ID 171477), (2) short-grain white rice cooked (FDC ID 169756), and (3) commercial curry sauce (FDC ID 173946), weighted to approximate a 500g plate composition of ~40% rice, ~30% chicken katsu, ~30% curry sauce. Input record values (energy 148 kcal/100g, protein 6.4g, fat 4.8g, carbohydrate 17.0g) are consistent with this weighted composite and are retained. Sodium 224mg/100g (1,120mg per 500g plate) is consistent with commercial Japanese curry roux sodium levels. All per-100g values from input record are preserved as they are internally consistent with USDA component-based estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — white rice (short-grain, Japanese-style, cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~70 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate source (~170g cooked rice per 500g plate). The curry roux (gelatinised wheat starch) contributes additional moderate-GI carbohydrate. The panko-crumbed fried chicken katsu contributes fat and protein that moderately slow gastric emptying. Composite dish GI estimated at ~70 (high). GL per 500g serving = GI/100 × available carbohydrate per serving = 70/100 × 85g = 59.5. Carbohydrate per serving: 17.0g/100g × 500g = 85g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~1,120mg per 500g plate) primarily from commercial Japanese curry roux blocks
hypertension.guidance: A single 500g plate of Japanese curry rice with chicken katsu provides approximately 1,120mg sodium — nearly 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000mg — driven predominantly by the commercial curry roux block; patients with hypertension should limit portion size, avoid additional high-sodium toppings such as fukujinzuke pickles, and prefer home-cooked versions using low-sodium roux blocks or scratch-cooked curry spices.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (GL ~59.5 per plate) from large volume of high-GI short-grain white rice combined with gelatinised roux starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 59.5 per standard plate — equivalent to consuming roughly 1.5 standard bowls of white rice — Japanese curry rice with chicken katsu is one of the highest-GL Japanese restaurant meals and is not recommended for patients with Type 2 Diabetes without significant carbohydrate modification; if consumed, patients should request a smaller rice portion or cauliflower rice substitute, consume the chicken katsu first to leverage protein-and-fat-mediated gastric emptying delay, and monitor postprandial glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.8g/100g; ~9g per plate) from deep-frying oil and curry roux, combined with high sodium (~1,120mg per plate)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of ~9g saturated fat and ~1,120mg sodium per plate places this dish in the moderate-to-high concern category for cardiovascular disease; the deep-frying oil used for the chicken katsu contributes oxidised lipids in commercial settings, and the high sodium load elevates blood pressure risk — patients with established cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption and pair it with a reduced rice portion to lower overall energy and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycemic load promotes postprandial hyperglycaemia-driven inflammation; commercial deep-frying may contribute oxidised lipids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The very high glycemic load (GL ~59.5) may promote acute postprandial inflammatory signalling via hyperglycaemia; while turmeric and cumin in the curry spice blend contain curcumin and polyphenols with theoretical anti-inflammatory properties, the quantities present in a roux-based Japanese curry are insufficient for clinically meaningful anti-inflammatory effect, and patients with rheumatoid arthritis should treat this dish as an occasional indulgence rather than a therapeutic food.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese Curry Rice with Chicken Katsu (チキンカツカレー, Chikin Katsu Karē) is one of Japan's most popular yoshoku (Western-influenced Japanese cuisine) comfort dishes, combining two iconic elements: a panko-crumbed deep-fried chicken cutlet (katsu) and a thick, mild, sweet Japanese curry sauce served over steamed short-grain white rice. The chicken katsu is prepared by pounding boneless chicken breast thin, coating it sequentially in wheat flour, beaten egg, and panko (Japanese breadcrumbs — larger, flakier, and crispier than Western breadcrumbs), then deep-frying until golden and crunchy. The Japanese curry sauce is characteristically made from commercial roux blocks (S&B or House Foods brands), which produce a thick, mildly spiced, slightly sweet sauce distinct from South Asian curries. Per 100g: approximately 148 kcal, 6.4g protein, 4.8g fat, 17.0g carbohydrate, 224mg sodium. Per standard 500g plate: approximately 740 kcal, 32g protein, 24g fat, 85g carbohydrate, 1,120mg sodium. Glycemic index ~70 (high); glycemic load ~59.5 per plate — one of the highest-GL Japanese restaurant meals due to the large volume of high-GI short-grain white rice combined with gelatinised roux starch. Clinically significant for Type 2 Diabetes (very high GL) and hypertension (high sodium from roux blocks). Available at CoCo Ichibanya (Singapore's dominant Japanese curry chain with approximately 20 outlets), Yoshinoya, and most Japanese restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Japanese curry rice with chicken katsu as a composite dish. Per-100g values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries (fried breaded chicken breast, short-grain white rice cooked, commercial curry sauce) weighted to approximate a standard 500g plate composition. Input record values are internally consistent with this component-based estimation and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact composite dish entry exists in any single approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Japanese curry rice dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 per input record, consistent with modern commercial cooking oils used in Japanese restaurant chains; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component entries for fried breaded chicken and commercial curry sauce. The input record cited Japan MEXT Standard Tables of Food Composition (8th Edition) as its source; this source is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central component-based estimation as the approved evidence basis. Per-100g values from the input record are retained as they are consistent with USDA component-based estimation. GI ~70 is an analogous estimate; the University of Sydney GI Database reports Japanese short-grain white rice at GI ~72, and the composite dish GI is estimated at ~70 accounting for the moderating effect of fat and protein from the chicken katsu and curry roux. GL per 500g serving = 70/100 × 85g available carbohydrate = 59.5. Two portion sizes are noted in the input record (500g and 600g); the 500g standard plate is used as the default serving for GL calculation. The 600g variant (220g rice + 180g chicken katsu + 200g curry sauce) represents a larger restaurant serving and would yield GL ~70.0 (70/100 × 100g carbohydrate at 600g × 17g/100g).
